access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME electronic text CME00087

rights.basis: The U-M CME collection applies the No Copyright–United States statement to its public-domain collection materials.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation

entities.description: A keyed electronic text of Horstmann's two poem transcriptions and their notes. The 2016 repository revision explicitly removed three prose fragments, leaving the poem-text selection rather than the complete article.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME electronic text CME00087

rights.basis: The U-M CME collection applies the No Copyright–United States statement to its public-domain collection materials.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation

entities.description: The edition-level selection formed by the two complete Middle English poem texts and retained textual notes, excluding Horstmann's two German introductions and later supplement.
entities.label: Celestin and Susanna poem-text selection

rights.basis: The two works and their manuscript witnesses are medieval and centuries outside copyright term; this does not grant rights in modern photographs, encodings, or editions.
rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English literary content

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English five-line-stanza saints' legend about a scholar who bargains with the devil, becomes pope, repents publicly, defeats the personified seven deadly sins, and is saved through divine and Marian mercy.
entities.label: Legend of Pope Celestin

entities.description: An anonymous alliterative Middle English stanzaic retelling of Susanna and the Elders. Five medieval witnesses are recorded; no direct descent among the surviving codices is asserted here.
entities.label: The Pistill of Susan

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna,' Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 1 (1878): 55–101; separate supplement 'Ein Beitrag zu Celestin,' 390–391.
bibliographic.title: Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna

entities.description: The immediate source edition. Horstmann prints two separate poems from two separate Bodleian manuscripts, each after a substantial German linguistic and metrical introduction.
entities.label: Carl Horstmann, Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna (1878)

rights.basis: The article was first published in 1878, before 1930.
rights.component: 1878 article, transcriptions, commentary, and typography

access.accessMethod: Browse the Internet Archive item or download the complete-volume PDF.
access.entityLabel: Internet Archive scan of Anglia volume 1

workPortion.label: CME00087 two-poem selection inside the complete carrier volume
notes: The target is discontiguous: Horstmann's Susanna introduction occupies printed pp. 85–93 between the two encoded poem texts.
The same complete volume retains the excluded article introductions and the separate pp. 390–391 supplement.

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463

access.accessMethod: The exact-shelfmark Digital Bodleian object has only f. 115r; no complete public codex was found.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463

access.accessMethod: Request a complete physical-codex reproduction from Bodleian Imaging Services, naming Celestin at current ff. 118v–124va separately.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463
notes: Specify both boards/covers, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, every manuscript leaf recto and verso, and edges or detached material when offered; do not request only the poem.
Ask separately about repository redistribution, public reference display, and commercial print-on-demand context before publishing supplied photographs.

rights.basis: Bodleian Imaging Services distinguishes noncommercial or academic use from publication and other licensed use; permission must be settled for the delivered files.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images supplied by Bodleian Imaging Services

entities.description: The unique known witness of Legend of Pope Celestin, at current ff. 118v–124va. Horstmann cites its older designation Laud L 70 and the opening as fol. 118b.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463

notes: DIMEV associates the manuscript language with LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 7750, Worcestershire; this is witness-level scribal evidence, not proof of the poem's composition place.

rights.basis: Bodleian Imaging Services distinguishes noncommercial or academic use from publication and other licensed use; permission must be settled for the delivered files.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images supplied by Bodleian Imaging Services

access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

entities.description: Horstmann's direct Susanna source at ff. 317ra–317vb. The late-fourteenth-century manuscript is physically incomplete, but its current complete published digital object exposes every cataloged surface in a 710-canvas sequence.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: Current catalog: viii + 342 + i leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413. Susanna occupies f. 317r–v.
physicalDescription.format: Large-format manuscript; Susanna is in three columns with stanzaic bracketing and red and blue decoration.

access.accessMethod: Browse the full Digital Bodleian object or use its IIIF manifest.
access.entityLabel: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of the Vernon Manuscript

localCopies.label: Complete Bodleian Vernon Manuscript IIIF bundle
notes: Exact hard-link reuse of the complete 710-canvas bundle; checksum and inode identity verified.
All canvases were retained at a 1600-pixel access profile.
Complete describes all canvases in the provider object; the medieval codex itself is historically incomplete.

workPortion.label: The Pistill of Susan in the full Vernon Manuscript
notes: The target occupies both sides of f. 317; the following Long Charter begins on the shared f. 317v canvas.

access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A II

rights.basis: British Library imaging terms and the quoted service govern delivery and publication of ordered photographs.
rights.component: Any supplied British Library photographs

dateStatements.label: target manuscript unit
dateStatements.value: second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A collateral, acephalous Susanna witness at current ff. 3r–5r. DIMEV states that stanzas 1–8 are absent because leaves are missing from the beginning of this manuscript unit; a nineteenth-century transcript is BL Egerton MS 2257, ff. 133r–136v.
entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A II

physicalDescription.extent: The current composite binding has 210 folios in two historical units; Susanna occupies ff. 3r–5r in the first unit.

rights.basis: British Library imaging terms and the quoted service govern delivery and publication of ordered photographs.
rights.component: Any supplied British Library photographs

access.entityLabel: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.818

entities.description: A collateral complete Susanna witness at ff. 1r–5r in a 54-leaf paper miscellany.
entities.label: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.818

physicalDescription.format: One column, 31–38 lines; Anglicana script; old vellum binding lined with canvas
notes: The item page gives England, Lincolnshire, while its prose note says northern England, possibly Yorkshire; both repository descriptions are retained without forcing a single composition claim.

access.accessMethod: Browse or individually download the complete 111-image published sequence.

access.format: 111 provider JPEG downloads bundled as ZIP

localCopies.label: Morgan Library MS M.818 complete published image inventory
notes: All 111 provider images were retained exactly as served: front cover, inside front cover, every manuscript leaf recto/verso, and inside back cover.
The provider sequence omits the spine and exterior lower cover; complete therefore means complete published image inventory, not complete exterior physical-object coverage.
The cache is for private scholarly research under Morgan terms and is not a redistributable corpus asset.

next_steps: Request the complete current physical codex with every surface, not only Celestin.
Name current ff. 118v–124va as the target mapping inside the full delivery.
Obtain written terms for local caching, Git-repository publication, noncommercial display, and commercial print-on-demand research context before redistributing any supplied image.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete color reproduction of Laud Misc. 463 be obtained and licensed for the project's private research and possible public-reference uses?

next_steps: Request each complete current physical object through BL Digitisation Services.
Map Susanna at Add MS 22283 ff. 125vb–126rc and Cotton Caligula A II ff. 3r–5r separately.
Confirm delivery, publication, repository-display, and commercial-use terms in writing.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color reproductions of Add MS 22283 and Cotton MS Caligula A II be obtained and licensed?

next_steps: Inspect the complete codex around the line-470 locus after a full facsimile is obtained.
Distinguish missing text in an exemplar from physical loss or scribal omission in the current codex.
openQuestions.question: Does codicological inspection of Laud Misc. 463 explain Horstmann's reported loss of one or two Celestin stanzas after line 470?

next_steps: Ask Imaging and Rights whether those exterior views already exist or may be commissioned.
Retain the present ZIP as complete only relative to the 111-image published inventory.
openQuestions.question: Can Morgan provide images of the M.818 spine and exterior lower cover omitted from the otherwise complete public image sequence?

lineage.summary: CME00087 is the checked-in copy of the University of Michigan encoding of two Middle English poem texts selected from Carl Horstmann's article 'Die Legenden von Celestin und Susanna,' Anglia 1 (1878). The encoding includes Legend of Pope Celestin on printed pages 67–85 and Susanna on pages 93–101, with Horstmann's textual notes, but omits his German Celestin introduction on pages 55–67, Susanna introduction on pages 85–93, and the separately printed metrical supplement on pages 390–391. Horstmann transcribed Celestin from the manuscript he called Laud L 70, now Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 463, current ff. 118v–124va, and Susanna from the Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 317ra–317vb. DIMEV records Celestin as a unique-witness poem and records five Susanna witnesses; the other four Susanna codices are collateral tradition, not sources of Horstmann's 1878 transcription. Complete public manuscript image sets are cached for Vernon and the collateral Morgan M.818 and Huntington HM 114 witnesses, with each work portion mapped inside the full published source. Laud Misc. 463 has only one unrelated public image, f. 115r; no complete public facsimile was found, so the whole physical codex and its target folios are specified separately in the Bodleian request route.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous poets
catalogSummary.date: Both poems predate their late-fourteenth-century witnesses; Horstmann's selected edition was published in 1878
catalogSummary.region: England; dialect evidence has been used to associate Celestin with the East Midlands and Susan with Yorkshire or northern England, but exact places of composition are unknown
catalogSummary.title: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The two Middle English verse works selected from Carl Horstmann's 1878 article and encoded together as CME00087: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan. This record does not describe Horstmann's complete article, Anglia volume 1, either complete manuscript, the larger five-witness Susanna tradition, or the University of Michigan encoding as intellectual works.

contentParts.label: Legend of Pope Celestin
notes: DIMEV records 147 five-line stanzas. CME's 146 LG count results from LG 44 containing ten lines and joining two literary stanzas.

Horstmann prints a note after line 470 stating that one or two stanzas of Envy's speech are missing. This is a textual lacuna in the transmitted poem, not proof of physical leaf loss in the present manuscript.
contentParts.summary: Celestin sells his soul to the devil in exchange for wisdom, becomes pope, sins against the faith, repents, confronts the seven deadly sins, receives help from Mary and angels, and voluntarily accepts execution as the completion of his penance.

contentParts.label: The Pistill of Susan
notes: DIMEV describes 28 thirteen-line ababababcdddc stanzas. Horstmann also prints a final two-line prayer. CME's 365 L count is one below the 366 printed lines because the short line 'So ffre' is encoded inside the preceding L element.

The Vernon Manuscript is the direct witness used by Horstmann. British Library Additional 22283, Cotton Caligula A II, Morgan M.818, and Huntington HM 114 are collateral witnesses and must not be placed on the direct CME lineage path.
contentParts.summary: Susanna rejects the sexual demands of two elders, who accuse her of adultery and secure her condemnation. Daniel separately questions the elders, exposes their incompatible claims about the tree beneath which the alleged act occurred, and vindicates Susanna.

dateStatements.display: Before the end of the fourteenth century; exact composition date unknown
notes: This is a terminus from the manuscript witness, not acceptance of Horstmann's stronger historical conjecture that the poem was more than a century older than the surviving copy.

dateStatements.display: End of the fourteenth century, in Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 463
Later fourteenth century; precise date unknown
notes: Peck describes the poem as a later-fourteenth-century adaptation; this dates the work, not any single manuscript copy.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century, represented by the Vernon and British Library Additional 22283 witnesses
notes: The statement deliberately does not force a single earliest witness where catalog date ranges overlap.

dateStatements.display: Published by Carl Horstmann in Anglia 1 (1878)
notes: This is the selected edition's publication date, not necessarily either poem's first-ever publication.

scope.description: Publication of the immediate source edition represented by CME00087

extent.description: Two selected poems in two XML DIV1 elements. Direct XML counts are 175 LG and 1,100 L: 146 LG/735 L for Celestin and 29 LG/365 L for Susan. These values describe the encoding; the literary structures are 147 five-line Celestin stanzas and 28 thirteen-line Susan stanzas plus a two-line prayer, with the XML discrepancies explained in the part records.

formStatements.description: Two stanzaic Middle English narrative poems; German prose introductions and editorial notes in Horstmann are not part of the selected work content.
DIMEV records 147 five-line aaabb stanzas; one CME LG container joins two stanzas and therefore yields 146 XML verse groups rather than 147 literary stanzas.

An alliterative poem in 28 thirteen-line ababababcdddc stanzas, followed in Horstmann by a two-line prayer. CME has 29 LG and 365 L because one short printed line is merged into its preceding L element.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML root also declares German because Horstmann's headings, notes, and editorial matter are German. German is edition-level paratext, not a language of either medieval work.

The direct source path is part-specific: Celestin derives from Laud Misc. 463 through Horstmann; Susan derives from the Vernon Manuscript through Horstmann. The other four Susan manuscripts are collateral witnesses, not additional upstream sources of CME00087.

Whole-manuscript facsimiles are preferred. The linked lineage record maps each poem's folios or canvases inside the complete cached manuscript object, records incomplete provider coverage where applicable, and distinguishes private research caches from redistributable assets.

Horstmann's italic type often represents expanded or supplied manuscript letters and words rather than semantic emphasis. His Celestin note explains that manuscript hooks or strokes on several letters were rendered as italic e, ampersand as italic and, and Jhu as Jesu; comparison against the manuscript remains necessary before reconstructing abbreviation marks.

next_steps: Consult current specialist work on DIMEV 352, Laud Misc. 463, dialect, rhyme, and the Celestin legend tradition.
Do not promote the witness's place of production or Horstmann's conjectured age to a place or date of composition without independent evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can Legend of Pope Celestin be dated or localized more securely than its late-fourteenth-century terminus and Horstmann's historical East Midlands rhyme analysis?

next_steps: Review current Huchown scholarship and textual comparisons rather than treating the chronicle title match as secure authorship.
Retain anonymous primary cataloging and disputed attributed authorship until the identification is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: Does Wyntoun's attribution of The Pistil of Suet Susane to Huchown identify the author of the surviving Middle English poem represented here?

next_steps: Request the complete current physical codex through Bodleian Imaging and identify ff. 118v–124va as the associated work portion.
Record any quotation, license, delivery URL, and exact file inventory in the lineage manifest before reuse.
openQuestions.question: Will the Bodleian supply a complete color facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 463, including all binding and blank surfaces, rather than only the Celestin folios?

next_steps: Use the British Library full-reproduction request route documented in the lineage manifest, requesting whole codices and separately naming the Susan folios.
Do not substitute selected public images that do not overlap the work.
openQuestions.question: Can complete current-volume facsimiles of British Library Additional 22283 and Cotton Caligula A II be obtained for the collateral Susan witnesses?

notes: Both works are Middle English. No precise place of composition is securely established.

assertion.notes: Horstmann's 1878 rhyme-based localization is historical dialect analysis, not direct evidence of where the poem was composed.
Peck reports that the dialect suggests southern Yorkshire while also describing the surviving dialect as mixed. This is not promoted to a certain place of composition.

notes: The broad northern association is consistent across the historical and modern sources consulted; surviving manuscripts contain scribal mixtures and do not independently prove origin.

assertion.notes: The two works are cataloged as the products of distinct anonymous poets; the disputed Huchown attribution applies only to Susan.

subjects.label: Celestin's sin, confession, penance, and salvation
The devil and the seven deadly sins

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English religious narratives. In Legend of Pope Celestin, Celestin bargains his soul to the devil for wisdom, rises to the papacy, repents publicly after preaching against the faith, confronts the personified seven deadly sins, and is saved through penitence and Marian intercession. In The Pistill of Susan, two elders falsely accuse Susanna after she rejects them; Daniel exposes their contradictory testimony, vindicates her, and brings the accusers to judgment.

CME00087 contains the two verse texts printed by Horstmann at pages 67–85 and 93–101. It excludes Horstmann's German introductions at pages 55–67 and 85–93 and excludes the separate supplement A Song to Our Lady at pages 390–391. The 2016 U-M revision history also records removal of three prose fragments, confirming that the encoding's intended content boundary is poem text rather than the complete article.

The XML's 175 LG and 1,100 L elements are encoding metrics, not a complete literary stanza count. Celestin has 147 five-line stanzas in DIMEV but 146 LG because one LG joins two stanzas. Susan's print structure has 28 thirteen-line stanzas plus a two-line prayer, but CME has 365 L because one short printed line is merged into the preceding L element.

assertion.notes: A project-supplied discovery title for the two-work CME unit; neither medieval poem has this collective title.

titles.value: Legend of Pope Celestin and The Pistill of Susan (Horstmann selection)

notes: The article title is useful edition-level access information but is broader than CME00087, which omits both German introductions and a later supplement.

scope.description: Horstmann's complete 1878 article, from which CME selects only the two poem texts

work.summary: Two anonymous Middle English religious narratives. In Legend of Pope Celestin, Celestin bargains his soul to the devil for wisdom, rises to the papacy, repents publicly after preaching against the faith, confronts the personified seven deadly sins, and is saved through penitence and Marian intercession. In The Pistill of Susan, two elders falsely accuse Susanna after she rejects them; Daniel exposes their contradictory testimony, vindicates her, and brings the accusers to judgment.
